text: Endurance training leads to improvements in running economy through a wide range of physiological responses including metabolic, cardiorespiratory, biomechanical, and neuromuscular adaptations.
Training history and accumulated training volume are important factors in improving running economy.
Uphill and level-ground high-intensity interval training may elicit further enhancements in running economy beyond those achieved through standard endurance training.

Short-term resistance and plyometric training improves running economy, likely through enhanced neuromuscular characteristics such as increased musculotendinous stiffness and improved motor unit recruitment.
Altitude acclimatization produces central and peripheral adaptations that improve oxygen delivery and utilization, which may improve running economy.

There is an optimal degree of flexibility and musculotendinous stiffness required to maximize running economy; excessive flexibility may impair economy while stretching remains valuable for injury prevention.
Dietary nitrates and caffeine are nutritional interventions that can reduce oxygen demand during submaximal running, thereby improving running economy.
